input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK

    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

briefing, and an authoritative investigation state containing the discovered map,
action coverage, inventory, clue text, provenance, and presentation history.

Your job is to challenge the proposed solution before it is committed. Treat clue
text as fact and the detective's conclusions as hypotheses that require support.

     evidence, and exonerating evidence for every cast member.

     and targeted presentations.
  5. Identify unsupported assumptions, fabricated clue IDs, duplicate clue IDs,
     and conclusions that merely repeat a clue instead of explaining its meaning.

are available, investigate it before finalizing. Prefer targeted tests: present an
item or clue when it contradicts testimony or plausibly connects a character to the
victim, scene, or means. Do not brute-force every possible pair, repeat completed
actions, or spend actions on low-value curiosity.

input.content:
You are the senior case reviewer. You receive a detective handoff, the original
briefing, and an authoritative investigation state containing the discovered map,
action coverage, inventory, clue text, provenance, and presentation history.

Your job is to challenge the proposed solution before it is committed. Treat clue
text as fact and the detective's conclusions as hypotheses that require support.

REVIEW METHOD:
  1. Build an evidence board. For every collected clue, state what it proves, which
     suspect or timeline claim it supports, and what testimony it contradicts.
  2. Build a suspect matrix. Evaluate motive, means, opportunity, inculpatory
     evidence, and exonerating evidence for every cast member.
  3. Audit every briefing timeline claim against collected evidence.
  4. Check investigation coverage: locations, searches, interviews, locked rooms,
     and targeted presentations.
  5. Identify unsupported assumptions, fabricated clue IDs, duplicate clue IDs,
     and conclusions that merely repeat a clue instead of explaining its meaning.

You have access to the investigation tools. If a high-value gap remains and actions
are available, investigate it before finalizing. Prefer targeted tests: present an
item or clue when it contradicts testimony or plausibly connects a character to the
victim, scene, or means. Do not brute-force every possible pair, repeat completed
actions, or spend actions on low-value curiosity.

Finish with a section titled REVISED HANDOFF containing:
  - the exact culprit_id and means_id;
  - one evidence note for every collected clue, using only clue IDs on the board;
  - a process explanation of at most 140 words with no clue IDs, covering who, how,
    why, the timeline, and why the other suspects are excluded.

The downstream parser sees your response, not the original detective's reasoning.
Make the revised handoff complete and unambiguous even if the original was wrong.

ORIGINAL BRIEFING
A body was found at dawn on the harbor docks â€” Aldric Marsh, a merchant known for his shipping business. The cause of death appears to be a blade wound. You have until sunrise to identify the killer before witnesses scatter and evidence disappears.

VALID CULPRIT IDS
  char_finn  (Deputy Finn)
  char_mara  (Mara Quinn)
  char_rex  (Rex Bowman)
  char_silas  (Silas Vane)

VALID MEANS IDS
  means_rope  (Rope)
  means_blade  (Knife)
  means_poison  (Poison)

TIMELINE CLAIMS
  step_1: Silas Vane brought Aldric Marsh to the riverside warehouse at midnight under the pretence of a cargo delivery.
  step_2: Silas Vane stabbed Aldric Marsh with his personal knife when the victim threatened to expose an illegal cargo run.

DETECTIVE HANDOFF
EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — Identifies the victim as Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This establishes his social circle and workplace, anchoring the murder to waterfront commerce and the dock network. Understanding his role clarifies why someone connected to shipments would target him and how access to the cargo route could be leveraged. It also situates the crime on the docks, where surveillance is sparse and cargo moves through a structured, signed system. The detail helps map possible alliances and rivalries within the port economy, guiding who might profit from his removal or fear exposure of illicit activity.

clue_02 — The post-mortem describes a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife. This narrows the weapon type to a precise class and supports close-quarters violence rather than a distant strike or poisoning. The wound profile serves as a forensic bridge to a weapon with similar characteristics, strengthening the link to the Engraved Knife later recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It also suggests the killer anticipated the victim’s movement and positioned themselves for an intimate, decisive blow, implying premeditation and a chosen tool that the killer would likely carry.

clue_03 — Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m., based on body temperature. This night-time window tightens the timeline and aligns with multiple other threads: Mara’s midnight sighting near the gates, and the midnight delivery ledger. The narrow window increases the plausibility of a premeditated plan rather than a random confrontation. It also constrains the alibis for suspects, pushing investigation toward those who could be active on the docks at that hour and who stand to gain from silencing Aldric.

clue_04 — The ledger shows a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorised and signed for by the harbormaster’s deputy, Silas Vane. This directly ties Silas to a midnight logistical operation involving the Riverside Warehouse, placing him at the locus of the crime at or near the window of death. The formal authorization indicates he controlled or supervised shipments that could be exploited to lure Aldric to the location, establishing a concrete mechanism for the supposed midnight delivery and setting the stage for a staged meeting.

clue_05 — The handwriting throughout this ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page. This consistency strengthens the inference that Silas was the signatory and likely the custodian of the night’s shipment, reducing the likelihood of forgery. The signature trail makes Silas a primary architect of the midnight delivery plan and a plausible person who could orchestrate Aldric Marsh’s appearance at the Riverside Warehouse, syncing with a motive tied to illicit imports and port corruption.

clue_06 — The blade profile of the found knife exactly matches the wound channel described in the post-mortem — same width and serration pattern. This forensic match is highly specific and ties the killing implement to the weapon recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It elevates the blade from generic tool to a personalized instrument of murder, implying the killer wanted a blade with a precise geometry. Combined with other items bearing the suspect’s initials, it narrows the set of plausible perpetrators to those who could possess such a blade and have access to both the victim and the crime scene.

clue_07 — The knife handle bears the initials “S.V.” engraved in brass on the pommel. This intimate, personalized mark directly associates the weapon with Silas Vane. In the context of a waterfront crime tied to midnight shipments, the engraved knife becomes a powerful physical link (or at least a strong circumstantial link) to Silas as the user or at least the owner of the murder blade. It shifts the narrative from a random stabbing to a crime embedded in Silas’s personal possessions and routines.

clue_08 — A signed debt receipt shows Rex Bowman cleared a substantial debt to Aldric Marsh three months ago. This introduces a potential financial motive structure in the victim’s circle, suggesting Aldric kept tabs on precarious debts and that someone who stood to lose from that debt settlement might stand to gain from his removal. While not proving guilt, it creates a motive landscape in which rivals and associates could be pressured to act, complicating the narrative and warranting consideration of Rex as a possible suspect with a debt-based grievance.

clue_09 — The bartender notes Rex Bowman came in agitated the night of the murder and drank heavily until closing. This behavior hints at an emotional state that could reflect stress, anger, or fear tied to Aldric’s death or the recent debt dynamics. While not conclusive, it corroborates Rex’s proximity and potential volatility on the night in question, sustaining a plausible line of inquiry around his state of mind and opportunity, particularly if Rex believed Aldric could have exposed or leveraged a debt or business disagreement to harm him.

clue_F1 — Deputy Finn states the body was found at first light near Pier 3 and had already been there for hours, with blood darkened. This places the victim at the scene well before dawn and confirms the waterfront location as a long-standing crime scene. It also supports the notion that the killer moved with caution and knowledge of the docks’ rhythms. Finn’s account anchors the scene and timing for subsequent cross-checks with alibis and the shipment timeline, helping to triangulate suspects who were active or close to Pier 3 near midnight or early dawn.

clue_F2 — Finn also notes Aldric Marsh had a reputation for clean dealings; the one exception being dock hands who thought he underpaid. This texture to the victim’s character hints at possible resentment or envy within the working dock community. While not a smoking gun, it broadens the field of potential suspects and motives beyond purely financial angles, suggesting that someone who felt wronged by Aldric’s business ethics or compensation structure might be motivated to silence him, either to protect a scheme or to punish perceived unfairness.

clue_M1 — Mara Quinn recalls seeing a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight, moving quickly as if trying to avoid attention. This is a key observational link to a potential assailant who could plausibly be Silas or another actor moving to the Riverside Warehouse at the critical moment. The limp detail introduces a physical descriptor that may map onto suspects’ usual mobility or disguise during the night, tightening the timeline and supporting the theory that the midnight encounter was an intentional lure rather than a chance meeting.

clue_R1 — Rex Bowman asserts he cleared a debt with Aldric three months prior, paid in full. This establishes a concrete financial tie and potential motive for rivalry or obstruction, indicating past friction but not necessarily direct involvement in the murder. It helps clarify the social network’s debts and favors, enabling the investigator to weigh whether financial leverage or vengeance among the victim’s business circle could have contributed to the crime, or whether Rex’s claim should be treated as a red herring to misdirect.

clue_R2 — Rex notes that Silas Vane has been secretive for weeks about who goes in and out of the warehouse at night, implying a suspicious pattern of avoiding transparency. This is a direct pointer to Silas’s behavior and a meaningful corroboration of a possible cover operation at the Riverside Warehouse. While not proof of guilt in itself, it aligns with other midnight-action indicators and with Silas’s potential control over shipments and access to restricted areas, strengthening the hypothesis that Silas exploited secrecy to stage or conceal the crime.

clue_S1 — Silas Vane claims he was at the office finishing month-end accounts all evening and left around ten, going straight home. This alibi is at odds with the midnight logistics and the delivery ledger tying him to the Riverside Warehouse at the relevant time. The discrepancy invites scrutiny: either his memory is faulty, or the alibi is a deliberate misdirection. In any case, it positions Silas as a central figure whose routine duties intersect with the crime window, making him the prime candidate to orchestrate a midnight scheme and to wield a personal blade against the victim if threatened with exposure.

clue_W1 — An official magistrate’s warrant authorized entry to any bonded warehouse on the dock, underscoring the legal pathway that permitted controlled access to the Riverside Warehouse. This demonstrates that the system allowed Silas to operate within a lawful framework to advance shipments or manage inventory, which could have been exploited to move Aldric into a compromising space. The warrant also supports the plausibility of coordinated, government-adjacent activity around illicit cargo, reinforcing the sense of a carefully constructed plan rather than a fit of passion.

CULPRIT: char_silas (Silas Vane), Means: means_blade (Knife)

PROCESS EXPLANATION:
The investigation centers on Silas Vane, the Harbormaster’s deputy, who supervised a midnight shipment to the riverside warehouse, tying him to both the crime scene and the timing of the murder. A signed midnight-delivery ledger implies he orchestrated a plan to lure Aldric Marsh to the Riverside Warehouse under the guise of an authorized shipment. The knife found at the warehouse bears Silas’s initials on the pommel, and the blade’s exact profile matches the killer’s wound, forming a direct physical link between Silas and the murder weapon. Mara’s midnight sighting suggests a high-risk activity on the docks during the window in question, and Rex’s and Silas’s own accounts about secrecy surrounding access to the warehouse provide motive- and opportunity-aligned context. Silas’s alibi, which places him away from midnight, is inconsistent with the consolidated timeline, while other suspects show no such direct weapon link or access. Taken together, the weight of signature, weapon, and logistics points to Silas as the killer who used his own blade to silence Aldric when the latter threatened exposure of an illegal cargo run, explaining the motive, method, and opportunity in a single, coherent narrative. Exonerations exist for Finn, Mara, and Rex based on lack of weapon linkage, absence of direct access at the critical moment, and inconsistent or unverifiable alibis. Finn’s discovery and testimony place him far from involvement, Mara’s midnight sighting does not prove participation, and Rex lacks the instrumental link to the weapon or the cargo operation that ties Silas to the act. The case closes with Silas’s involvement in orchestrating a midnight delivery, using his own blade in a calculated confrontation that ended Aldric Marsh’s life at the Riverside Warehouse.

AUTHORITATIVE INVESTIGATION STATE

input.content:
You are the senior case reviewer. You receive a detective handoff, the original
briefing, and an authoritative investigation state containing the discovered map,
action coverage, inventory, clue text, provenance, and presentation history.

Your job is to challenge the proposed solution before it is committed. Treat clue
text as fact and the detective's conclusions as hypotheses that require support.

REVIEW METHOD:
  1. Build an evidence board. For every collected clue, state what it proves, which
     suspect or timeline claim it supports, and what testimony it contradicts.
  2. Build a suspect matrix. Evaluate motive, means, opportunity, inculpatory
     evidence, and exonerating evidence for every cast member.
  3. Audit every briefing timeline claim against collected evidence.
  4. Check investigation coverage: locations, searches, interviews, locked rooms,
     and targeted presentations.
  5. Identify unsupported assumptions, fabricated clue IDs, duplicate clue IDs,
     and conclusions that merely repeat a clue instead of explaining its meaning.

You have access to the investigation tools. If a high-value gap remains and actions
are available, investigate it before finalizing. Prefer targeted tests: present an
item or clue when it contradicts testimony or plausibly connects a character to the
victim, scene, or means. Do not brute-force every possible pair, repeat completed
actions, or spend actions on low-value curiosity.

Finish with a section titled REVISED HANDOFF containing:
  - the exact culprit_id and means_id;
  - one evidence note for every collected clue, using only clue IDs on the board;
  - a process explanation of at most 140 words with no clue IDs, covering who, how,
    why, the timeline, and why the other suspects are excluded.

The downstream parser sees your response, not the original detective's reasoning.
Make the revised handoff complete and unambiguous even if the original was wrong.

ORIGINAL BRIEFING
A body was found at dawn on the harbor docks â€” Aldric Marsh, a merchant known for his shipping business. The cause of death appears to be a blade wound. You have until sunrise to identify the killer before witnesses scatter and evidence disappears.

VALID CULPRIT IDS
  char_finn  (Deputy Finn)
  char_mara  (Mara Quinn)
  char_rex  (Rex Bowman)
  char_silas  (Silas Vane)

VALID MEANS IDS
  means_rope  (Rope)
  means_blade  (Knife)
  means_poison  (Poison)

TIMELINE CLAIMS
  step_1: Silas Vane brought Aldric Marsh to the riverside warehouse at midnight under the pretence of a cargo delivery.
  step_2: Silas Vane stabbed Aldric Marsh with his personal knife when the victim threatened to expose an illegal cargo run.

DETECTIVE HANDOFF
EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — Identifies the victim as Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This establishes his social circle and workplace, anchoring the murder to waterfront commerce and the dock network. Understanding his role clarifies why someone connected to shipments would target him and how access to the cargo route could be leveraged. It also situates the crime on the docks, where surveillance is sparse and cargo moves through a structured, signed system. The detail helps map possible alliances and rivalries within the port economy, guiding who might profit from his removal or fear exposure of illicit activity.

clue_02 — The post-mortem describes a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife. This narrows the weapon type to a precise class and supports close-quarters violence rather than a distant strike or poisoning. The wound profile serves as a forensic bridge to a weapon with similar characteristics, strengthening the link to the Engraved Knife later recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It also suggests the killer anticipated the victim’s movement and positioned themselves for an intimate, decisive blow, implying premeditation and a chosen tool that the killer would likely carry.

clue_03 — Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m., based on body temperature. This night-time window tightens the timeline and aligns with multiple other threads: Mara’s midnight sighting near the gates, and the midnight delivery ledger. The narrow window increases the plausibility of a premeditated plan rather than a random confrontation. It also constrains the alibis for suspects, pushing investigation toward those who could be active on the docks at that hour and who stand to gain from silencing Aldric.

clue_04 — The ledger shows a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorised and signed for by the harbormaster’s deputy, Silas Vane. This directly ties Silas to a midnight logistical operation involving the Riverside Warehouse, placing him at the locus of the crime at or near the window of death. The formal authorization indicates he controlled or supervised shipments that could be exploited to lure Aldric to the location, establishing a concrete mechanism for the supposed midnight delivery and setting the stage for a staged meeting.

clue_05 — The handwriting throughout this ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page. This consistency strengthens the inference that Silas was the signatory and likely the custodian of the night’s shipment, reducing the likelihood of forgery. The signature trail makes Silas a primary architect of the midnight delivery plan and a plausible person who could orchestrate Aldric Marsh’s appearance at the Riverside Warehouse, syncing with a motive tied to illicit imports and port corruption.

clue_06 — The blade profile of the found knife exactly matches the wound channel described in the post-mortem — same width and serration pattern. This forensic match is highly specific and ties the killing implement to the weapon recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It elevates the blade from generic tool to a personalized instrument of murder, implying the killer wanted a blade with a precise geometry. Combined with other items bearing the suspect’s initials, it narrows the set of plausible perpetrators to those who could possess such a blade and have access to both the victim and the crime scene.

clue_07 — The knife handle bears the initials “S.V.” engraved in brass on the pommel. This intimate, personalized mark directly associates the weapon with Silas Vane. In the context of a waterfront crime tied to midnight shipments, the engraved knife becomes a powerful physical link (or at least a strong circumstantial link) to Silas as the user or at least the owner of the murder blade. It shifts the narrative from a random stabbing to a crime embedded in Silas’s personal possessions and routines.

clue_08 — A signed debt receipt shows Rex Bowman cleared a substantial debt to Aldric Marsh three months ago. This introduces a potential financial motive structure in the victim’s circle, suggesting Aldric kept tabs on precarious debts and that someone who stood to lose from that debt settlement might stand to gain from his removal. While not proving guilt, it creates a motive landscape in which rivals and associates could be pressured to act, complicating the narrative and warranting consideration of Rex as a possible suspect with a debt-based grievance.

clue_09 — The bartender notes Rex Bowman came in agitated the night of the murder and drank heavily until closing. This behavior hints at an emotional state that could reflect stress, anger, or fear tied to Aldric’s death or the recent debt dynamics. While not conclusive, it corroborates Rex’s proximity and potential volatility on the night in question, sustaining a plausible line of inquiry around his state of mind and opportunity, particularly if Rex believed Aldric could have exposed or leveraged a debt or business disagreement to harm him.

clue_F1 — Deputy Finn states the body was found at first light near Pier 3 and had already been there for hours, with blood darkened. This places the victim at the scene well before dawn and confirms the waterfront location as a long-standing crime scene. It also supports the notion that the killer moved with caution and knowledge of the docks’ rhythms. Finn’s account anchors the scene and timing for subsequent cross-checks with alibis and the shipment timeline, helping to triangulate suspects who were active or close to Pier 3 near midnight or early dawn.

clue_F2 — Finn also notes Aldric Marsh had a reputation for clean dealings; the one exception being dock hands who thought he underpaid. This texture to the victim’s character hints at possible resentment or envy within the working dock community. While not a smoking gun, it broadens the field of potential suspects and motives beyond purely financial angles, suggesting that someone who felt wronged by Aldric’s business ethics or compensation structure might be motivated to silence him, either to protect a scheme or to punish perceived unfairness.

clue_M1 — Mara Quinn recalls seeing a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight, moving quickly as if trying to avoid attention. This is a key observational link to a potential assailant who could plausibly be Silas or another actor moving to the Riverside Warehouse at the critical moment. The limp detail introduces a physical descriptor that may map onto suspects’ usual mobility or disguise during the night, tightening the timeline and supporting the theory that the midnight encounter was an intentional lure rather than a chance meeting.

clue_R1 — Rex Bowman asserts he cleared a debt with Aldric three months prior, paid in full. This establishes a concrete financial tie and potential motive for rivalry or obstruction, indicating past friction but not necessarily direct involvement in the murder. It helps clarify the social network’s debts and favors, enabling the investigator to weigh whether financial leverage or vengeance among the victim’s business circle could have contributed to the crime, or whether Rex’s claim should be treated as a red herring to misdirect.

clue_R2 — Rex notes that Silas Vane has been secretive for weeks about who goes in and out of the warehouse at night, implying a suspicious pattern of avoiding transparency. This is a direct pointer to Silas’s behavior and a meaningful corroboration of a possible cover operation at the Riverside Warehouse. While not proof of guilt in itself, it aligns with other midnight-action indicators and with Silas’s potential control over shipments and access to restricted areas, strengthening the hypothesis that Silas exploited secrecy to stage or conceal the crime.

clue_S1 — Silas Vane claims he was at the office finishing month-end accounts all evening and left around ten, going straight home. This alibi is at odds with the midnight logistics and the delivery ledger tying him to the Riverside Warehouse at the relevant time. The discrepancy invites scrutiny: either his memory is faulty, or the alibi is a deliberate misdirection. In any case, it positions Silas as a central figure whose routine duties intersect with the crime window, making him the prime candidate to orchestrate a midnight scheme and to wield a personal blade against the victim if threatened with exposure.

clue_W1 — An official magistrate’s warrant authorized entry to any bonded warehouse on the dock, underscoring the legal pathway that permitted controlled access to the Riverside Warehouse. This demonstrates that the system allowed Silas to operate within a lawful framework to advance shipments or manage inventory, which could have been exploited to move Aldric into a compromising space. The warrant also supports the plausibility of coordinated, government-adjacent activity around illicit cargo, reinforcing the sense of a carefully constructed plan rather than a fit of passion.

CULPRIT: char_silas (Silas Vane), Means: means_blade (Knife)

PROCESS EXPLANATION:
The investigation centers on Silas Vane, the Harbormaster’s deputy, who supervised a midnight shipment to the riverside warehouse, tying him to both the crime scene and the timing of the murder. A signed midnight-delivery ledger implies he orchestrated a plan to lure Aldric Marsh to the Riverside Warehouse under the guise of an authorized shipment. The knife found at the warehouse bears Silas’s initials on the pommel, and the blade’s exact profile matches the killer’s wound, forming a direct physical link between Silas and the murder weapon. Mara’s midnight sighting suggests a high-risk activity on the docks during the window in question, and Rex’s and Silas’s own accounts about secrecy surrounding access to the warehouse provide motive- and opportunity-aligned context. Silas’s alibi, which places him away from midnight, is inconsistent with the consolidated timeline, while other suspects show no such direct weapon link or access. Taken together, the weight of signature, weapon, and logistics points to Silas as the killer who used his own blade to silence Aldric when the latter threatened exposure of an illegal cargo run, explaining the motive, method, and opportunity in a single, coherent narrative. Exonerations exist for Finn, Mara, and Rex based on lack of weapon linkage, absence of direct access at the critical moment, and inconsistent or unverifiable alibis. Finn’s discovery and testimony place him far from involvement, Mara’s midnight sighting does not prove participation, and Rex lacks the instrumental link to the weapon or the cargo operation that ties Silas to the act. The case closes with Silas’s involvement in orchestrating a midnight delivery, using his own blade in a calculated confrontation that ended Aldric Marsh’s life at the Riverside Warehouse.

briefing, and an authoritative investigation state containing the discovered map,
action coverage, inventory, clue text, provenance, and presentation history.

Your job is to challenge the proposed solution before it is committed. Treat clue
text as fact and the detective's conclusions as hypotheses that require support.

  2. Build a suspect matrix. Evaluate motive, means, opportunity, inculpatory
     evidence, and exonerating evidence for every cast member.
  3. Audit every briefing timeline claim against collected evidence.
  4. Check investigation coverage: locations, searches, interviews, locked rooms,
     and targeted presentations.
  5. Identify unsupported assumptions, fabricated clue IDs, duplicate clue IDs,
     and conclusions that merely repeat a clue instead of explaining its meaning.

You have access to the investigation tools. If a high-value gap remains and actions
are available, investigate it before finalizing. Prefer targeted tests: present an
item or clue when it contradicts testimony or plausibly connects a character to the
victim, scene, or means. Do not brute-force every possible pair, repeat completed
actions, or spend actions on low-value curiosity.

input.content:
You are the senior case reviewer. You receive a detective handoff, the original
briefing, and an authoritative investigation state containing the discovered map,
action coverage, inventory, clue text, provenance, and presentation history.

Your job is to challenge the proposed solution before it is committed. Treat clue
text as fact and the detective's conclusions as hypotheses that require support.

REVIEW METHOD:
  1. Build an evidence board. For every collected clue, state what it proves, which
     suspect or timeline claim it supports, and what testimony it contradicts.
  2. Build a suspect matrix. Evaluate motive, means, opportunity, inculpatory
     evidence, and exonerating evidence for every cast member.
  3. Audit every briefing timeline claim against collected evidence.
  4. Check investigation coverage: locations, searches, interviews, locked rooms,
     and targeted presentations.
  5. Identify unsupported assumptions, fabricated clue IDs, duplicate clue IDs,
     and conclusions that merely repeat a clue instead of explaining its meaning.

You have access to the investigation tools. If a high-value gap remains and actions
are available, investigate it before finalizing. Prefer targeted tests: present an
item or clue when it contradicts testimony or plausibly connects a character to the
victim, scene, or means. Do not brute-force every possible pair, repeat completed
actions, or spend actions on low-value curiosity.

Finish with a section titled REVISED HANDOFF containing:
  - the exact culprit_id and means_id;
  - one evidence note for every collected clue, using only clue IDs on the board;
  - a process explanation of at most 140 words with no clue IDs, covering who, how,
    why, the timeline, and why the other suspects are excluded.

The downstream parser sees your response, not the original detective's reasoning.
Make the revised handoff complete and unambiguous even if the original was wrong.

ORIGINAL BRIEFING
A body was found at dawn on the harbor docks â€” Aldric Marsh, a merchant known for his shipping business. The cause of death appears to be a blade wound. You have until sunrise to identify the killer before witnesses scatter and evidence disappears.

VALID CULPRIT IDS
  char_finn  (Deputy Finn)
  char_mara  (Mara Quinn)
  char_rex  (Rex Bowman)
  char_silas  (Silas Vane)

VALID MEANS IDS
  means_rope  (Rope)
  means_blade  (Knife)
  means_poison  (Poison)

TIMELINE CLAIMS
  step_1: Silas Vane brought Aldric Marsh to the riverside warehouse at midnight under the pretence of a cargo delivery.
  step_2: Silas Vane stabbed Aldric Marsh with his personal knife when the victim threatened to expose an illegal cargo run.

DETECTIVE HANDOFF
EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — Identifies the victim as Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This establishes his social circle and workplace, anchoring the murder to waterfront commerce and the dock network. Understanding his role clarifies why someone connected to shipments would target him and how access to the cargo route could be leveraged. It also situates the crime on the docks, where surveillance is sparse and cargo moves through a structured, signed system. The detail helps map possible alliances and rivalries within the port economy, guiding who might profit from his removal or fear exposure of illicit activity.

clue_02 — The post-mortem describes a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife. This narrows the weapon type to a precise class and supports close-quarters violence rather than a distant strike or poisoning. The wound profile serves as a forensic bridge to a weapon with similar characteristics, strengthening the link to the Engraved Knife later recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It also suggests the killer anticipated the victim’s movement and positioned themselves for an intimate, decisive blow, implying premeditation and a chosen tool that the killer would likely carry.

clue_03 — Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m., based on body temperature. This night-time window tightens the timeline and aligns with multiple other threads: Mara’s midnight sighting near the gates, and the midnight delivery ledger. The narrow window increases the plausibility of a premeditated plan rather than a random confrontation. It also constrains the alibis for suspects, pushing investigation toward those who could be active on the docks at that hour and who stand to gain from silencing Aldric.

clue_04 — The ledger shows a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorised and signed for by the harbormaster’s deputy, Silas Vane. This directly ties Silas to a midnight logistical operation involving the Riverside Warehouse, placing him at the locus of the crime at or near the window of death. The formal authorization indicates he controlled or supervised shipments that could be exploited to lure Aldric to the location, establishing a concrete mechanism for the supposed midnight delivery and setting the stage for a staged meeting.

clue_05 — The handwriting throughout this ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page. This consistency strengthens the inference that Silas was the signatory and likely the custodian of the night’s shipment, reducing the likelihood of forgery. The signature trail makes Silas a primary architect of the midnight delivery plan and a plausible person who could orchestrate Aldric Marsh’s appearance at the Riverside Warehouse, syncing with a motive tied to illicit imports and port corruption.

clue_06 — The blade profile of the found knife exactly matches the wound channel described in the post-mortem — same width and serration pattern. This forensic match is highly specific and ties the killing implement to the weapon recovered at the Riverside Warehouse. It elevates the blade from generic tool to a personalized instrument of murder, implying the killer wanted a blade with a precise geometry. Combined with other items bearing the suspect’s initials, it narrows the set of plausible perpetrators to those who could possess such a blade and have access to both the victim and the crime scene.

clue_07 — The knife handle bears the initials “S.V.” engraved in brass on the pommel. This intimate, personalized mark directly associates the weapon with Silas Vane. In the context of a waterfront crime tied to midnight shipments, the engraved knife becomes a powerful physical link (or at least a strong circumstantial link) to Silas as the user or at least the owner of the murder blade. It shifts the narrative from a random stabbing to a crime embedded in Silas’s personal possessions and routines.

clue_08 — A signed debt receipt shows Rex Bowman cleared a substantial debt to Aldric Marsh three months ago. This introduces a potential financial motive structure in the victim’s circle, suggesting Aldric kept tabs on precarious debts and that someone who stood to lose from that debt settlement might stand to gain from his removal. While not proving guilt, it creates a motive landscape in which rivals and associates could be pressured to act, complicating the narrative and warranting consideration of Rex as a possible suspect with a debt-based grievance.

clue_09 — The bartender notes Rex Bowman came in agitated the night of the murder and drank heavily until closing. This behavior hints at an emotional state that could reflect stress, anger, or fear tied to Aldric’s death or the recent debt dynamics. While not conclusive, it corroborates Rex’s proximity and potential volatility on the night in question, sustaining a plausible line of inquiry around his state of mind and opportunity, particularly if Rex believed Aldric could have exposed or leveraged a debt or business disagreement to harm him.

clue_F1 — Deputy Finn states the body was found at first light near Pier 3 and had already been there for hours, with blood darkened. This places the victim at the scene well before dawn and confirms the waterfront location as a long-standing crime scene. It also supports the notion that the killer moved with caution and knowledge of the docks’ rhythms. Finn’s account anchors the scene and timing for subsequent cross-checks with alibis and the shipment timeline, helping to triangulate suspects who were active or close to Pier 3 near midnight or early dawn.

clue_F2 — Finn also notes Aldric Marsh had a reputation for clean dealings; the one exception being dock hands who thought he underpaid. This texture to the victim’s character hints at possible resentment or envy within the working dock community. While not a smoking gun, it broadens the field of potential suspects and motives beyond purely financial angles, suggesting that someone who felt wronged by Aldric’s business ethics or compensation structure might be motivated to silence him, either to protect a scheme or to punish perceived unfairness.

clue_M1 — Mara Quinn recalls seeing a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight, moving quickly as if trying to avoid attention. This is a key observational link to a potential assailant who could plausibly be Silas or another actor moving to the Riverside Warehouse at the critical moment. The limp detail introduces a physical descriptor that may map onto suspects’ usual mobility or disguise during the night, tightening the timeline and supporting the theory that the midnight encounter was an intentional lure rather than a chance meeting.

clue_R1 — Rex Bowman asserts he cleared a debt with Aldric three months prior, paid in full. This establishes a concrete financial tie and potential motive for rivalry or obstruction, indicating past friction but not necessarily direct involvement in the murder. It helps clarify the social network’s debts and favors, enabling the investigator to weigh whether financial leverage or vengeance among the victim’s business circle could have contributed to the crime, or whether Rex’s claim should be treated as a red herring to misdirect.

clue_R2 — Rex notes that Silas Vane has been secretive for weeks about who goes in and out of the warehouse at night, implying a suspicious pattern of avoiding transparency. This is a direct pointer to Silas’s behavior and a meaningful corroboration of a possible cover operation at the Riverside Warehouse. While not proof of guilt in itself, it aligns with other midnight-action indicators and with Silas’s potential control over shipments and access to restricted areas, strengthening the hypothesis that Silas exploited secrecy to stage or conceal the crime.

clue_S1 — Silas Vane claims he was at the office finishing month-end accounts all evening and left around ten, going straight home. This alibi is at odds with the midnight logistics and the delivery ledger tying him to the Riverside Warehouse at the relevant time. The discrepancy invites scrutiny: either his memory is faulty, or the alibi is a deliberate misdirection. In any case, it positions Silas as a central figure whose routine duties intersect with the crime window, making him the prime candidate to orchestrate a midnight scheme and to wield a personal blade against the victim if threatened with exposure.
